title: Thyroid Cancer & Thyroid Nodule Workup
oneLiner: Systematic evaluation of thyroid nodules to stratify malignancy risk, prevent missed cancer, and manage acute airway obstruction.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute airway obstruction** – Stridor + tracheal deviation + hoarseness = rapidly growing mass (anaplastic carcinoma, large goiter, haemorrhage into cyst). Requires immediate ENT/anaesthetics, CT neck/chest, and IV steroids.
- **Thyroid storm** (from hyperfunctioning nodule) – Fever >38°C, tachycardia out of proportion, altered mental state. Needs beta-blocker (propranolol), thionamide (carbimazole), and steroids. Single screen: bedside ECG shows sinus tachycardia or atrial fibrillation.
- **Invasive lymphoma or anaplastic carcinoma** – Rapidly enlarging neck mass (<6 weeks) with dysphagia, neck pain, and stridor – urgent MDT referral and airway assessment.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Palpable neck lump (often incidental on imaging or self-detected)
  - Dysphagia, hoarseness (recurrent laryngeal nerve compression), cough, neck discomfort
  - Rapid growth (weeks) ± cervical lymphadenopathy (worrisome for malignancy)
  - Hyperthyroid symptoms (sweating, palpitations, weight loss) if toxic nodule
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Thyroid nodule >1 cm – assess consistency (hard/fixed), mobility (tethered?), contour
  - Check for cervical lymph nodes (central level VI, lateral II–IV)
  - Tracheal deviation, stridor, voice change (listen to voice, ask about swallowing)
  - Signs of hyperthyroidism (tremor, sweaty palms, eye signs if Graves')
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → Benign nodule** (colloid nodule, thyroid cyst, adenoma) – soft, mobile, no nodes, stable over months, low-risk on ultrasound (EU-TIRADS 1–2)
- **Less likely → Malignant nodule** (papillary, follicular, medullary, anaplastic, lymphoma) – hard, irregular, fixed, rapid growth, cervical nodes, hoarseness, high-risk on ultrasound (EU-TIRADS 4–5)
- **Splitter:** Ultrasound feature – microcalcifications + irregular margins = high risk (TIRADS 5, biopsy). Spongiform appearance = low risk (TIRADS 2, no biopsy unless >2 cm). Do not rely on palpation alone.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Neck examination (document size, consistency, nodes)
  - Voice check (ask to speak – hoarseness suggests RLN involvement)
  - Pulse, temp, signs of thyrotoxicosis
- **Bloods:**
  - **TSH, FT4, FT3** – if low TSH → radionuclide scan (toxic nodule rare malignant)
  - **Calcitonin** (if family history of MEN2, or suspicious ultrasound for medullary)
  - **Calcium, albumin** – baseline before surgery; TPO antibodies if suspicion of Hashimoto's
  - Consider: serum thyroglobulin (not diagnostic alone, used post-surgery)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Thyroid ultrasound with TIRADS scoring (use EU-TIRADS or ACR-TIRADS)
  - **Second-line:** CT neck/chest with contrast – if compressive symptoms, retrosternal extension, or lymphadenopathy (for pre-operative planning)
- **Special tests:**
  - **FNA (fine-needle aspiration)** – Bethesda classification (I–VI) to guide surgery
  - Core needle biopsy if FNA non-diagnostic (Bethesda I)
  - Medullary carcinoma – RET mutation testing, serum calcitonin, MEN2 screening
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - TIRADS categories: EU-TIRADS 1 (benign) → no biopsy; 2 (low) → >2 cm consider FNA; 3 (intermediate) → >1.5 cm; 4 (high) → >1 cm; 5 (very high) → >0.5 cm or any suspicious node
  - Bethesda: I – non-diagnostic, II – benign, III – AUS/FLUS, IV – follicular neoplasm, V – suspicious, VI – malignant
- **Never-miss test:** **Thyroid ultrasound + TIRADS scoring** in any thyroid nodule >1 cm. **Calcitonin** if family history or suspicious features – missing medullary carcinoma delays curative surgery.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If airway compromise: sit patient up, call ENT/anaesthetics, O₂, IV dexamethasone 8–16 mg STAT, urgent CT neck/chest, prepare for difficult intubation or tracheostomy
  - If thyroid storm: propranolol (IV or PO) – but check heart rate, carbimazole loading, hydrocortisone, cooling
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Refer to **thyroid MDT** (endocrine surgeon, endocrinologist, radiologist, pathologist)
  - Discuss FNA results (Bethesda category) ± core biopsy
  - If toxic nodule (low TSH, hot on scan): refer for radioiodine or surgery; start beta-blocker if symptomatic
  - Do **not** start levothyroxine suppression pre-operatively – wait for MDT decision
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Differentiated thyroid cancer (papillary/follicular): total or hemithyroidectomy based on risk; post-op radioiodine ablation for high-risk (if N1, T3/4, aggressive histology)
  - Medullary: total thyroidectomy + central/lateral neck dissection; surveillance with calcitonin and CEA
  - Anaplastic/lymphoma: urgent MDT; may need palliative radiotherapy or chemotherapy
  - Post-op: levothyroxine suppression therapy (TSH goal <0.1 mU/L for high-risk, 0.5–2.0 for low-risk); check thyroglobulin/antibodies at 6–12 months
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - For sepsis/obstruction: NBM initially, then regular diet if safe; IV fluids (0.9% NaCl 1–2 L/day) if nil by mouth
  - VTE prophylaxis if immobile or surgery planned
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly, escalate if airway concerns; pulse and temp if thyrotoxic
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Hyperthyroidism control (thyrotoxicosis from toxic nodule):**
  - **Carbimazole** – loading: 20–40 mg PO once daily (then titrate). Check FBC before starting (risk of agranulocytosis).
  - **Propranolol** – 40–80 mg PO TDS (or 1–2 mg IV slowly if severe). Avoid in asthma.
- **Airway oedema (compressive obstruction):**
  - **Dexamethasone** – 8–16 mg IV STAT. [check local policy]
- **Post-operative suppression:**
  - **Levothyroxine** – dose determined by TSH: start 1.6–1.8 mcg/kg/day PO (adjust every 6 weeks). [check local policy]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Carbimazole: avoid in patients with known agranulocytosis (check FBC); interactions with warfarin.
  - Propranolol: avoid in asthma, use caution in heart block.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Not typically required for these doses.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Starting carbimazole without baseline FBC – missing agranulocytosis. Patients must be warned: stop drug and get urgent FBC if sore throat, fever, or mouth ulcers.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Airway obstruction from tumour/haematoma (post-FNA or rapid growth)
  - Thyroid storm (rare, from hyperfunctioning nodule or post-operative in uncontrolled hyperthyroidism)
  - FNA site haematoma ± voice change (usually self-limiting)
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury (hoarseness)
  - Hypoparathyroidism (after total thyroidectomy) – check calcium, PTH
  - Hypothyroidism (after surgery or radioiodine)
  - Recurrent thyroid cancer (5–20% depending on stage)
  - Radioiodine side effects: sialadenitis, second malignancies (very low risk)
sections.Prognosis:
- **Differentiated thyroid cancer** (papillary/follicular) – 5-year survival >95% for localized disease; excellent even with nodal mets. Risk increases with age >55, T3/4, N1b, M1.
- **Medullary** – 5-year survival ~85% if confined to thyroid, ~40% if distant mets.
- **Anaplastic** – median survival 3–6 months; aggressive palliation.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always use TIRADS on ultrasound** – saves unnecessary FNA in low-risk nodules (e.g., spongiform, simple cysts).
- **Check calcitonin** in any patient with family history of MEN2, thyroid nodule, or suspicious ultrasound features (coarse calcifications) – early detection of medullary carcinoma improves prognosis.
- **Document voice before any biopsy or surgery** – baseline voice assessment (ask to speak, consider laryngoscopy if hoarse). This helps medicolegal protection if RLN injury occurs.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not biopsy a low-risk nodule** (<1.5 cm with TIRADS 2 or spongiform) – results in overdiagnosis and unnecessary surgery.
- **Do not start levothyroxine suppression pre-operatively** – it can mask disease markers (thyroglobulin) and complicate post-surgical risk stratification.
- **Do not ignore a rapidly enlarging neck mass** – assume anaplastic or lymphoma until proven otherwise. Delay in airway assessment is deadly.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Difficulty breathing, voice change, or rapid swelling over hours
  - Signs of thyrotoxicosis (palpitations, tremor, weight loss, diarrhoea)
  - Post-FNA: large haematoma, expanding neck swelling, dysphagia
  - Any “sore throat” or fever >38°C if on carbimazole – stop drug, get urgent FBC
- **Follow-up:**
  - Low-risk nodule (TIRADS 1–2): repeat ultrasound in 12 months
  - High-risk nodule (TIRADS 4–5): referral to thyroid MDT within 2 weeks; FNA results in 1–2 weeks
  - Post-surgery: endocrinology clinic at 4–6 weeks for levothyroxine adjustment; thyroglobulin/antibodies at 6 months

rawMarkdown:
## Thyroid Cancer & Thyroid Nodule Workup
**One-liner:** Systematic evaluation of thyroid nodules to stratify malignancy risk, prevent missed cancer, and manage acute airway obstruction.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute airway obstruction** – Stridor + tracheal deviation + hoarseness = rapidly growing mass (anaplastic carcinoma, large goiter, haemorrhage into cyst). Requires immediate ENT/anaesthetics, CT neck/chest, and IV steroids.
- **Thyroid storm** (from hyperfunctioning nodule) – Fever >38°C, tachycardia out of proportion, altered mental state. Needs beta-blocker (propranolol), thionamide (carbimazole), and steroids. Single screen: bedside ECG shows sinus tachycardia or atrial fibrillation.
- **Invasive lymphoma or anaplastic carcinoma** – Rapidly enlarging neck mass (<6 weeks) with dysphagia, neck pain, and stridor – urgent MDT referral and airway assessment.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Palpable neck lump (often incidental on imaging or self-detected)
  - Dysphagia, hoarseness (recurrent laryngeal nerve compression), cough, neck discomfort
  - Rapid growth (weeks) ± cervical lymphadenopathy (worrisome for malignancy)
  - Hyperthyroid symptoms (sweating, palpitations, weight loss) if toxic nodule
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Thyroid nodule >1 cm – assess consistency (hard/fixed), mobility (tethered?), contour
  - Check for cervical lymph nodes (central level VI, lateral II–IV)
  - Tracheal deviation, stridor, voice change (listen to voice, ask about swallowing)
  - Signs of hyperthyroidism (tremor, sweaty palms, eye signs if Graves')

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → Benign nodule** (colloid nodule, thyroid cyst, adenoma) – soft, mobile, no nodes, stable over months, low-risk on ultrasound (EU-TIRADS 1–2)
- **Less likely → Malignant nodule** (papillary, follicular, medullary, anaplastic, lymphoma) – hard, irregular, fixed, rapid growth, cervical nodes, hoarseness, high-risk on ultrasound (EU-TIRADS 4–5)
- **Splitter:** Ultrasound feature – microcalcifications + irregular margins = high risk (TIRADS 5, biopsy). Spongiform appearance = low risk (TIRADS 2, no biopsy unless >2 cm). Do not rely on palpation alone.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Neck examination (document size, consistency, nodes)
  - Voice check (ask to speak – hoarseness suggests RLN involvement)
  - Pulse, temp, signs of thyrotoxicosis
- **Bloods:**
  - **TSH, FT4, FT3** – if low TSH → radionuclide scan (toxic nodule rare malignant)
  - **Calcitonin** (if family history of MEN2, or suspicious ultrasound for medullary)
  - **Calcium, albumin** – baseline before surgery; TPO antibodies if suspicion of Hashimoto's
  - Consider: serum thyroglobulin (not diagnostic alone, used post-surgery)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Thyroid ultrasound with TIRADS scoring (use EU-TIRADS or ACR-TIRADS)
  - **Second-line:** CT neck/chest with contrast – if compressive symptoms, retrosternal extension, or lymphadenopathy (for pre-operative planning)
- **Special tests:**
  - **FNA (fine-needle aspiration)** – Bethesda classification (I–VI) to guide surgery
  - Core needle biopsy if FNA non-diagnostic (Bethesda I)
  - Medullary carcinoma – RET mutation testing, serum calcitonin, MEN2 screening
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - TIRADS categories: EU-TIRADS 1 (benign) → no biopsy; 2 (low) → >2 cm consider FNA; 3 (intermediate) → >1.5 cm; 4 (high) → >1 cm; 5 (very high) → >0.5 cm or any suspicious node
  - Bethesda: I – non-diagnostic, II – benign, III – AUS/FLUS, IV – follicular neoplasm, V – suspicious, VI – malignant
- **Never-miss test:** **Thyroid ultrasound + TIRADS scoring** in any thyroid nodule >1 cm. **Calcitonin** if family history or suspicious features – missing medullary carcinoma delays curative surgery.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If airway compromise: sit patient up, call ENT/anaesthetics, O₂, IV dexamethasone 8–16 mg STAT, urgent CT neck/chest, prepare for difficult intubation or tracheostomy
  - If thyroid storm: propranolol (IV or PO) – but check heart rate, carbimazole loading, hydrocortisone, cooling
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Refer to **thyroid MDT** (endocrine surgeon, endocrinologist, radiologist, pathologist)
  - Discuss FNA results (Bethesda category) ± core biopsy
  - If toxic nodule (low TSH, hot on scan): refer for radioiodine or surgery; start beta-blocker if symptomatic
  - Do **not** start levothyroxine suppression pre-operatively – wait for MDT decision
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Differentiated thyroid cancer (papillary/follicular): total or hemithyroidectomy based on risk; post-op radioiodine ablation for high-risk (if N1, T3/4, aggressive histology)
  - Medullary: total thyroidectomy + central/lateral neck dissection; surveillance with calcitonin and CEA
  - Anaplastic/lymphoma: urgent MDT; may need palliative radiotherapy or chemotherapy
  - Post-op: levothyroxine suppression therapy (TSH goal <0.1 mU/L for high-risk, 0.5–2.0 for low-risk); check thyroglobulin/antibodies at 6–12 months
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - For sepsis/obstruction: NBM initially, then regular diet if safe; IV fluids (0.9% NaCl 1–2 L/day) if nil by mouth
  - VTE prophylaxis if immobile or surgery planned
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly, escalate if airway concerns; pulse and temp if thyrotoxic

### Drugs & Doses
- **Hyperthyroidism control (thyrotoxicosis from toxic nodule):**
  - **Carbimazole** – loading: 20–40 mg PO once daily (then titrate). Check FBC before starting (risk of agranulocytosis).
  - **Propranolol** – 40–80 mg PO TDS (or 1–2 mg IV slowly if severe). Avoid in asthma.
- **Airway oedema (compressive obstruction):**
  - **Dexamethasone** – 8–16 mg IV STAT. [check local policy]
- **Post-operative suppression:**
  - **Levothyroxine** – dose determined by TSH: start 1.6–1.8 mcg/kg/day PO (adjust every 6 weeks). [check local policy]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Carbimazole: avoid in patients with known agranulocytosis (check FBC); interactions with warfarin.
  - Propranolol: avoid in asthma, use caution in heart block.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Not typically required for these doses.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Starting carbimazole without baseline FBC – missing agranulocytosis. Patients must be warned: stop drug and get urgent FBC if sore throat, fever, or mouth ulcers.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Airway obstruction from tumour/haematoma (post-FNA or rapid growth)
  - Thyroid storm (rare, from hyperfunctioning nodule or post-operative in uncontrolled hyperthyroidism)
  - FNA site haematoma ± voice change (usually self-limiting)
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury (hoarseness)
  - Hypoparathyroidism (after total thyroidectomy) – check calcium, PTH
  - Hypothyroidism (after surgery or radioiodine)
  - Recurrent thyroid cancer (5–20% depending on stage)
  - Radioiodine side effects: sialadenitis, second malignancies (very low risk)

### Prognosis
- **Differentiated thyroid cancer** (papillary/follicular) – 5-year survival >95% for localized disease; excellent even with nodal mets. Risk increases with age >55, T3/4, N1b, M1.
- **Medullary** – 5-year survival ~85% if confined to thyroid, ~40% if distant mets.
- **Anaplastic** – median survival 3–6 months; aggressive palliation.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always use TIRADS on ultrasound** – saves unnecessary FNA in low-risk nodules (e.g., spongiform, simple cysts).
- **Check calcitonin** in any patient with family history of MEN2, thyroid nodule, or suspicious ultrasound features (coarse calcifications) – early detection of medullary carcinoma improves prognosis.
- **Document voice before any biopsy or surgery** – baseline voice assessment (ask to speak, consider laryngoscopy if hoarse). This helps medicolegal protection if RLN injury occurs.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not biopsy a low-risk nodule** (<1.5 cm with TIRADS 2 or spongiform) – results in overdiagnosis and unnecessary surgery.
- **Do not start levothyroxine suppression pre-operatively** – it can mask disease markers (thyroglobulin) and complicate post-surgical risk stratification.
- **Do not ignore a rapidly enlarging neck mass** – assume anaplastic or lymphoma until proven otherwise. Delay in airway assessment is deadly.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Difficulty breathing, voice change, or rapid swelling over hours
  - Signs of thyrotoxicosis (palpitations, tremor, weight loss, diarrhoea)
  - Post-FNA: large haematoma, expanding neck swelling, dysphagia
  - Any “sore throat” or fever >38°C if on carbimazole – stop drug, get urgent FBC
- **Follow-up:**
  - Low-risk nodule (TIRADS 1–2): repeat ultrasound in 12 months
  - High-risk nodule (TIRADS 4–5): referral to thyroid MDT within 2 weeks; FNA results in 1–2 weeks
  - Post-surgery: endocrinology clinic at 4–6 weeks for levothyroxine adjustment; thyroglobulin/antibodies at 6 months

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/ENT urgently:**
  - Stridor,
